request.question: For Which team did the second man to win a championship in three different decades play ?

title: Eloise Jarvis McGraw
Mark Jonathan Harris
Eastern Shore League
Bobby Unser
Tim Duncan
Graeme Hick
John Salley
Gene Upshaw
Herb Thomas (baseball)
Duran Duran discography
sentences: Eloise Jarvis McGraw (December 9, 1915 – November 30, 2000) was an American author of children's books and young adult novels.
She was awarded the Newbery Honor three times in three different decades, for her novels "Moccasin Trail" (1952), "The Golden Goblet" (1962), and "The Moorchild" (1997). "
A Really Weird Summer" (1977) won an Edgar Award for Best Juvenile Mystery from the Mystery Writers of America.
McGraw had a very strong interest in history, and among the many books she wrote for children are "Greensleeves", "The Seventeenth Swap", and "Mara, Daughter of the Nile".
A Lewis Carroll Shelf Award was given to "Moccasin Trail" in 1963.
Mark Jonathan Harris (born 1941) is an American documentary filmmaker probably best known for his films "" (2000) and "The Long Way Home" (1997).
He has directed three documentaries which have gone on to win Oscars, across three different decades.
The Eastern Shore Baseball League was a class D minor league baseball league that operated on the Delmarva Peninsula for parts of three different decades.
The league's first season was in 1922 and the last was in 1949, although the years were not consecutive, and featured teams from Maryland, Delaware, and Virginia.
The first incarnation lasted from 1922 to mid-1928 (disbanded in July), the second from 1937–41, and the third from 1946–49.
Though the level of play was competitive and many future major leaguers gained experience in the ESBL, funding the league remained a constant problem for the rural franchises.
Robert William "Bobby" Unser (born February 20, 1934) is an American former automobile racer.
He is the brother of Al Unser, Jerry Unser and Louis Unser, the father of Robby Unser, and the uncle of Al Unser, Jr. and Johnny Unser.
He is one of ten drivers to win the Indianapolis 500 three or more times, and one of only two (followed by Rick Mears) to have won the 500 in three different decades (1968, 75, 81).
Bobby has also been a spokesman and advocate of many commercial products.

Timothy Theodore Duncan (born April 25, 1976) is an American retired professional basketball player who played his entire 19-year career with the San Antonio Spurs of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
Widely considered to be the greatest power forward of all time, he is a five-time NBA champion, two-time NBA MVP, three-time NBA Finals MVP, NBA All-Star Game MVP and NBA Rookie of the Year.
He is also a 15-time NBA All-Star and the only player to be selected to both the All-NBA and All-Defensive Teams in all of his first 13 seasons.
Graeme Ashley Hick MBE (born 23 May 1966) is a former English cricketer who played 65 Test matches and 120 One Day Internationals for England.

He played county cricket for Worcestershire for his entire English domestic career, a period of well over twenty years, and in 2008 he surpassed Graham Gooch's record for the most matches in all forms of the game combined.

He scored more than 40,000 first-class runs, mostly from number three in the order, and he is one of only three players to have passed 20,000 runs in List A cricket (Graham Gooch and Sachin Tendulkar are the others) and is one of only twenty-five players to have scored 100 centuries in first-class cricket.
He is the only cricketer who scored first-class triple hundreds in three different decades (1988, 1997 and 2002).
He is the second highest run scorer of all time after Graham Gooch.

Despite these achievements, he is commonly held to have underachieved in international cricket, a view based on comparison of Hick's overall first-class batting average of 52.23 "vis-à-vis" his Test average of 31.32.
John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964) is an American retired professional basketball player, actor and talk show host.

He was the first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises, as well as the first player (and only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan) in the NBA to win a championship in three different decades.

Eugene Thurman Upshaw Jr. (August 15, 1945 – August 20, 2008), also known as "Uptown Gene", was an American football player for the Oakland Raiders of the American Football League (AFL) and later the National Football League (NFL).
He later served as the executive director of the National Football League Players' Association (NFLPA).
In 1987, he was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame.
He is also the only player in NFL history to reach the Super Bowl in three different decades with the same team.
Herbert Mark Thomas (May 26, 1902 in Sampson City, Florida – December 4, 1991 in Starke, Florida) was a right-handed minor and major league baseball player and minor league manager.
Although his major league career lasted only three seasons, 1924, 1925 and 1927, for two different teams, the Boston Braves and New York Giants, his minor league career spanned 17 seasons.
He played professionally in three different decades.

British band Duran Duran have released fourteen studio albums, three remix albums, two live albums, four compilation albums, two box sets, five EPs, thirty-nine singles, fourteen video albums on various record labels. Duran Duran have sold over 100 million records.

The band is one of the few artists to achieve US top 10 albums in three different decades (1980s, 1990s and 2010s) and one of even fewer to reach the top 20 in all four decades of their existence: five albums in the 1980s ("Duran Duran", "Rio", "Seven and the Ragged Tiger", "Arena" and "Notorious"), three albums in the 1990s ("Liberty", "Duran Duran" [aka "The Wedding Album"] and "Thank You"), one album in the 2000s ("Astronaut") and one album in the 2010s ("Paper Gods").

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.

Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: For Which team did the second man to win a championship in three different decades play ?
hop1_docs: Indian cricket team in Zimbabwe in 2005 | The Indian cricket team toured Zimbabwe for cricket matches in August and September 2005.  The Indians played in the Videocon Tri-Series in Zimbabwe, winning three of five matches but losing the final to finish second in the three-team tournament, and they played Zimbabwe for two Test matches in September 2005.  The hosts Zimbabwe were ninth out of ten teams in the ICC Test Championship, with their last win against the top eight Test nations coming in June 2001, against India.  They did not manage to win a match here either, as India, led by the performances of fast bowler Irfan Pathan (who took 21 wickets), won the series 2–0 after an innings win in the first Test and a 10-wicket win in the second.  India consolidated their third place in the Test championship with the win, which was also their first series win in Zimbabwe in four attempts.

John Salley | John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964) is an American retired professional basketball player, actor and talk show host.  He was the first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises, as well as the first player (and only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan) in the NBA to win a championship in three different decades.

2008 Dutch Caribbean Age Group Championships | 2008 Dutch Caribbean Age Group Championships is a championship for young Track & Field athletes of the islands of the Dutch Kingdom.  In 2008 there were three island competing.  Aruba, Curaçao and Sint Maarten.  They compete in different events and the athlete with the overall most points will be declared the winner.  There are two age groups, they are 10–11 years (Pentathlon) and 12–13 years (Heptathlon).  This year was the first year that hurdles and baseball throw was added to the event list for the heptathlon.  Sint Eustatius did not have a complete team.  They only competed with a full Heptathlon team.  Like every year, the hosting island is allowed to have two teams (which was Aruba).  There were 32 athletes competing in total and Curaçao was declared the overall winner, Aruba came in second and Sint Maarten came in third.  This would also mark the 4th win for Curaçao

1894–95 British Home Championship | The 1894-95 British Home Championship was an international football tournament played between the British Home Nations.  The competition was won by England, who like second placed Wales, did not lose a game.  Wales however failed to win one either, scoring three draws and so finishing behind England.  Scotland took joint second place with three points gained from a win, draw and a loss.  Ireland came last with a single point garnered from their draw with Wales.

1920–21 British Home Championship | The 1920-21 British Home Championship was a football tournament played between the British Home Nations during the 1920–21 season.  The second tournament played since the hiatus of the First World War, the 1921 competition was dominated by Scotland, who won the first of seven championships they would claim throughout the decade.  England and reigning champions Wales came joint second as goal difference was not at this stage used to separate teams.

1978–79 British Home Championship | The 1978-79 British Home Championship was a British Home Nations competition, won by the English football side and notable for seeing marked increases in hooliganism and falling attendance which would result in its cancellation in 1984.  The English started well, beating Northern Ireland to match the heavy Welsh victory over Scotland on the same day, which featured a hat trick by John Toshack.  Scotland recovered by beating the Irish in their next match while England and Wales played out a goalless draw, leaving three sides theoretically capable of winning the Championship in the final round.  Wales could only manage a draw with the Irish and so in the deciding match between England and Scotland, a 1–1 half time score gave the Scots some hope but a strong second half performance from England was rewarded with a deserved 3–1 win.  This result gave England the Championship, with Wales in second place.  The tournament also saw the introduction of goal difference to separate teams, although it had no effect on the eventual outcome.

1955–56 British Home Championship | The 1955-56 British Home Championship was a football tournament played during the 1955–56 season between the British Home Nations.  It was the only occasion during the hundred-year run of the Home Championship in which all four teams finished level on points.  As goal difference was not used to determine position until 1979, all four teams shared the trophy, holding it for three months each.  Had goal difference been used to determine the winner, then England would have won with Scotland second.

response.summary_1:
- Fact: [2] — John Salley is described as "the first player ... to win a championship in three different decades" (quote: "first player ... to win a championship in three different decades").
- Fact: [2] — Passage names the other player as Tim Duncan (quote: "the other being Tim Duncan").
- Bridge entity: "the second man to win a championship in three different decades" -> candidate: Tim Duncan (mentioned in [2]). The identity as "second" is not explicitly clarified in the passages (ambiguous whether "second" means chronologically second or simply "the other" listed).
- Missing / ambiguous facts preventing an answer:
  1) No passage here gives which team Tim Duncan played for.
  2) Championship years for Tim Duncan (to confirm the three different decades and chronological ordering) are not present.
  3) Championship years and franchises for John Salley (to verify who was first vs. second chronologically) are not present in detail.
- Next-hop retrieval targets (prioritized):
  1) Fetch the Tim Duncan (Wikipedia) page — extract lead/infobox "Career history" / "Teams" and the list of NBA championship years (e.g., 1999, 2003, 2005, 2007, 2014) or a sentence stating he "won championships in three different decades." This will directly answer which team he played for.
  2) Fetch the John Salley (Wikipedia) page — extract the list of championship years and the franchises he won with (to confirm chronological order who was first/second).
  3) If needed, fetch a list page (e.g., "List of NBA players with championships in three different decades" or disambiguation) to confirm there are only these two players and establish ordering. 

No final answer provided because the passages do not state which team the "second man" (Tim Duncan) played for nor the championship years to confirm ordering.
response.hop2_query: Tim Duncan teams played for and championship years
hop2_docs: Twin Towers (San Antonio Spurs) | The Twin Towers is a name applied to the combination of Tim Duncan and David Robinson playing as the frontcourt of the San Antonio Spurs from 1997–2003.  Both players were selected first overall by the San Antonio Spurs in their draft years with Robinson's selection coming in the 1987 NBA draft and Duncan's selection coming ten years later and both played their entire careers with the San Antonio Spurs.

Tim Duncan (American football) | Tim Duncan (born June 12, 1979) is a former American football placekicker who played in the National Football League (NFL) for the Arizona Cardinals.  He played college football at the University of Oklahoma.  Duncan also played for Kentucky State University in the division II SIAC conference during the 1997 season when he helped the Thoroughbreds win the inaugural Pioneer Bowl.  He transferred to Oklahoma the following season.

Tim Duncan | Timothy Theodore Duncan (born April 25, 1976) is an American retired professional basketball player who played his entire 19-year career with the San Antonio Spurs of the National Basketball Association (NBA).  Widely considered to be the greatest power forward of all time, he is a five-time NBA champion, two-time NBA MVP, three-time NBA Finals MVP, NBA All-Star Game MVP and NBA Rookie of the Year.  He is also a 15-time NBA All-Star and the only player to be selected to both the All-NBA and All-Defensive Teams in all of his first 13 seasons.

John Salley | John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964) is an American retired professional basketball player, actor and talk show host.  He was the first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises, as well as the first player (and only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan) in the NBA to win a championship in three different decades.

2011 Harvard Crimson football team | The 2011 Harvard Crimson football team represented Harvard University in the 2011 NCAA Division I FCS football season.  The Crimson were led by 18th year head coach Tim Murphy and played their home games at Harvard Stadium.  They are a member of the Ivy League.  They finished the season 9–1, 7–0 in Ivy League play to claim the conference championship.
Tim Kennelly | Tim Kennelly (6 July 1954 – 6 December 2005) was Irish Gaelic footballer.  His league and championship career with the Kerry senior team spanned ten years from 1974 to 1984.

2012 Fresno State Bulldogs football team | The 2012 Fresno State Bulldogs football team represented California State University, Fresno in the 2012 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The Bulldogs were led by first-year head coach Tim DeRuyter and played their home games at Bulldog Stadium.  This was their first year as a member of the Mountain West Conference.  They finished the season 9–4, 7–1 in Mountain West play to share the conference championship with Boise State and San Diego State.  They were invited to the Hawaii Bowl where they were defeated by SMU.
response.summary_2: The second man is Tim Duncan. Tim Duncan played his entire 19-year NBA career with the San Antonio Spurs (passage [3]), and passage [4] lists him as the other player—alongside John Salley—who won championships in three different decades.
